findings.title: Empty-regular-file fast path on POSIX uses stale `file_store.mode` and never fires for fresh Bun.file()
findings.problem: After `resolve_size_and_last_modified` has fstat'd the fd and set `self.could_block = !is_regular_file(stat.st_mode)`, the empty-file short-circuit tests `bun_sys::is_regular_file(self.file_store.mode)`. `self.file_store` is a clone taken in `create_with_ctx` from the Blob's store *before* any stat, and `File::init` defaults `mode = 0`. `is_regular_file(0)` → `kind_from_mode(0) == Unknown` → false, so for `Bun.file(path).text()` on a 0-byte file the fast path is never taken: we fall through, allocate a 16-byte buffer, zero the 64 KB stack buffer, and issue a read() that returns 0. The Windows equivalent (line 1262) correctly uses `this.is_regular_file` which was set from the fresh fstat at line 1240.
findings.evidence: read_file.rs:738 `if self.size == 0 && bun_sys::is_regular_file(self.file_store.mode)` — but `file_store` is populated at read_file.rs:346 `let file_store = store.data.as_file().clone();` and `resolve_size_and_last_modified` (lines 672-722) never writes `self.file_store.mode`; it only sets `self.could_block` from `stat.st_mode`. `File::default()` at src/jsc/webcore_types.rs:783 sets `mode: 0`. Compare ReadFileUV at line 1262: `if this.size == 0 && this.is_regular_file` (correct — `is_regular_file` set at line 1240 from fresh fstat).
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the condition with `if self.size == 0 && !self.could_block` (which is exactly `is_regular_file(fresh_stat.st_mode)`), mirroring the Windows branch. This restores the intended short-circuit: skip buffer alloc and read() for 0-byte regular files.
findings.user_visible_impact: `await Bun.file(emptyFile).text()` / `.bytes()` on Linux/macOS does an unnecessary 16-byte heap allocation, a 64 KB memset, and one extra read() syscall per call. In build-tool / glob workloads that touch many empty placeholder files, each such read pays full overhead instead of returning immediately after fstat.

findings.title: Fast-path guard for stat'd Blob destinations is inverted (checks ==RegularFile instead of !=)
findings.problem: `fast_path_ok` intends (per the original Zig comment: 'Is this a file that is known to be a pipe? Let's avoid blocking the main thread on it.') to SKIP the main-thread sync fast path when the destination is a known pipe/TTY. The code instead skips it when the destination is a known REGULAR FILE: `f.mode != 0 && bun_core::kind_from_mode(f.mode) == bun_core::FileKind::File`. The condition is inverted. Result: a Blob whose mode has been populated (Bun.stdout/Bun.stderr, or any Bun.file() after `.size`/`.stat()`) and is a regular file is forced onto the async threadpool even for <256 KiB writes, while a Blob known to be a pipe/TTY takes the synchronous path the comment was trying to avoid. Bug originates in Zig PR #7470 and was ported faithfully.
findings.evidence: Blob.rs:5102-5107 vs. the Zig source (`git show b1c8ae97ff -- src/bun.js/webcore/blob.zig`) which carries the comment 'known to be a pipe' immediately above `bun.isRegularFile(...)`. RareData::stdout()/stderr() (src/jsc/rare_data.rs:907-934) fstat and set `mode`, so Bun.stdout/Bun.stderr always hit the guarded branch.
findings.proposed_fix: Flip the comparison to `!= bun_core::FileKind::File` (equivalently `!bun_sys::is_regular_file(f.mode)`), so the sync fast path is skipped only when the destination is known NOT to be a regular file.
findings.user_visible_impact: `Bun.write(Bun.stdout, shortBuf)` when stdout is redirected to a file (e.g. `bun run script.js > out`) pays threadpool-dispatch + promise-microtask latency (≈10–20 µs) instead of a ~1 µs synchronous write; any `const f=Bun.file(p); await f.size; await Bun.write(f, small)` pattern likewise loses the fast path.

findings.title: EAGAIN retry in WriteFile::do_write never re-issues the write() — infinite busy-loop
findings.problem: `do_write` computes `let result = sys::write(fd, ...)` at line 347 *outside* the `loop { match &result { ... } }` at line 350. On `Err` with errno == RETRY (EAGAIN) and `!self.could_block`, it executes `continue` (line 361), which re-matches the SAME stale `result` forever — the syscall is never retried. `could_block` is computed from `file.pathlike.is_fd()` (write_file.rs:462) so any path-opened destination has `could_block=false`; if that path resolves to a FIFO/pipe/char-device opened O_NONBLOCK and the kernel returns EAGAIN, the threadpool worker spins at 100% CPU. The identical bug exists in the pre-port Zig (`const result = bun.sys.write(...)` outside `while (true)`), so it is latent but real.
findings.evidence: write_file.rs:347-373: `let result = sys::write(...); loop { match &result { ... Err(err) => { if err.get_errno()==io::RETRY { if !self.could_block { continue; } ... } } } break; }`. `result` is never reassigned inside the loop body.
findings.proposed_fix: Move the `sys::write(fd, ...)` call inside the loop (making `result` mutable), or replace `continue` with `result = sys::write(fd, &self.bytes_blob.shared_view()[off..off+len]); continue;` so EAGAIN actually retries the syscall.
findings.user_visible_impact: `Bun.write(Bun.file('/tmp/my_fifo'), bigBuf)` (or any path that resolves to a non-regular file) hangs a threadpool worker at 100% CPU once the pipe buffer fills, instead of waiting for writability; user sees the promise never resolve and one core pegged.

findings.title: response.text() skips the to_blob_if_possible() fast path that json()/arrayBuffer()/bytes() take for already-buffered streams
findings.problem: In BodyMixin::get_text, the `Value::Locked` + readable-stream branch goes straight to `locked.set_promise(.., Action::GetText, Some(readable))`, which dispatches into C++ `readableStreamToText` (JS property `get`, `JSC::call`, promise-then plumbing in BunStreamConsumers.cpp:875-911). Every sibling — get_json (line 1896), get_array_buffer (line 1946), get_bytes (line 2001), get_blob (line 2154) — first calls `value.to_blob_if_possible()`, which for a Locked body with a native ByteStream/ByteBlobLoader that already holds the full payload (ReadableStream.rs:194-203) converts to `Value::InternalBlob` and falls through to the synchronous `use_as_any_blob_* → to_*` path. `get_text` is the only consumer missing this call, so `.text()` on a Response/Request whose body is a completed native stream (e.g. `new Response(stream)` constructed from a Blob- or Bytes-backed ReadableStream, or after `.body` was touched) always takes the slow C++ stream-consumer path while `.json()` on the same object resolves synchronously in Rust.
findings.proposed_fix: Add `value.to_blob_if_possible();` before the `if let Value::Locked(locked) = value` check in `get_text` (both the with-readable and no-readable Locked arms), mirroring get_json/get_array_buffer/get_bytes. Then the already-buffered case falls through to `use_as_any_blob_allow_non_utf8_string() → AnyBlob::to_string(Transfer)`.
findings.user_visible_impact: `.text()` on a Response/Request backed by a native ReadableStream whose data is already buffered (e.g. `new Response(blobStream).text()` or `.text()` after touching `.body`) takes the C++ readableStreamToText path (handle->get + JSC::call + promise chaining) instead of the zero-JS-call native path. A microbenchmark of `new Response(someCompletedByteStream)` would show `.text()` measurably slower than `.json()`/`.arrayBuffer()` for the same payload, even though `.text()` should be the cheapest of the three.

findings.title: Per-write version bump defeats the cached row Structure for every statement on the DB
findings.problem: After every sqlite3_step(), Bun does `if (!sqlite3_stmt_readonly(stmt)) castedThis->version_db->version++;` and then checks `need_update()` (== `version_db->version != this->version`). Any INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE therefore increments the shared DB version, which makes `need_update()` return true for *every* prepared statement on that DB on its next execution, forcing `initializeColumnNames()` to run again. That function heap-allocates a fresh `PropertyNameArrayBuilder`, clears `_structure`, calls `sqlite3_column_name` / `String::fromUTF8ReplacingInvalidSequences` / `Identifier::fromString` per column, and rebuilds the JSC Structure via `addPropertyTransition`. The intent (commit bcc4580 "invalidate column name caches when the schema of table may change") was to catch ALTER TABLE, but plain DML does not change column names — so the expensive fast-path cache (`_structure` used by `putDirectOffset` in `constructResultObject`) is thrown away on every write. Worse, because the increment happens *before* the `need_update()` check, the write statement itself rebuilds its own (usually empty) column set on every `.run()`.
findings.proposed_fix: Only bump `version_db->version` for schema-changing statements. A cheap accurate check is available via SQLite: cache `sqlite3_column_count(stmt)` on the statement and compare after step (SQLite auto-reprepares on SQLITE_SCHEMA so the count/name changes become visible), or gate the bump on `sqlite3_stmt_readonly(stmt)==0 && sqlite3_stmt_isexplain(stmt)==0 && sqlite3_column_count(stmt)==0 && <statement touches sqlite_schema>`. Simplest correct fix: drop the unconditional `version++` on every write; instead, in `initializeColumnNames`, skip the rebuild when `sqlite3_column_count` and the first column name still match the cache. At minimum, move `update_version()` before the increment so a write statement doesn't invalidate *itself* every call.
findings.user_visible_impact: In the canonical ORM pattern `for each row { insert.run(row); }` followed by or interleaved with `select.all()`, every `select.all()` pays the full cost of re-deriving column Identifiers and re-creating the JSC Structure (one heap alloc + N UTF-8 decodes + N Structure transitions) instead of reusing the cache, and every `insert.run()` pays an extra heap alloc for a throwaway PropertyNameArrayBuilder. This adds measurable overhead proportional to column count on every query in any workload that mixes reads and writes on the same Database.

findings.title: Statement#run() with no args allocates the {changes, lastInsertRowid} object twice
findings.problem: For statements with `paramsCount > 0`, `this.run` is wired to `#run`. When the user calls `.run()` with no arguments (re-executing with previously bound params), `#run` calls `this.#runNoArgs()` — which already builds and returns a `{changes, lastInsertRowid}` object — then discards that return value and calls `createChangesObject()` a second time. Two objects are allocated where one suffices.
findings.proposed_fix: Change the zero-arg branch of `#run` to `return this.#runNoArgs();`.
findings.user_visible_impact: One extra `{changes, lastInsertRowid}` allocation (and two extra internal-field reads) per `.run()` call on parameterized statements invoked without arguments.

findings.title: onwrite `needTick` tests kObjectMode bit instead of kDestroyed (operator-precedence bug preserved with Number())
findings.problem: The expression `state[kState] & Number(kDestroyed !== 0)` evaluates to `state[kState] & 1`, which is the kObjectMode bit (1<<0), not kDestroyed (1<<4). The original Node.js line `(state[kState] & kDestroyed !== 0)` has the same precedence bug; Bun wrapped it in `Number()` (likely to silence a lint/TS warning) instead of fixing the parenthesization. Result: for objectMode Writables with a synchronous `_write` and no user callback (e.g. every objectMode Transform/PassThrough), `needTick` is always truthy, so the fast-path `state.pendingcb--` is never taken on the first write of each tick and a `process.nextTick(afterWrite, ...)` is scheduled instead. Conversely, for destroyed non-objectMode streams the needed afterWrite tick is skipped.
findings.evidence: kObjectMode = 1<<0 and kDestroyed = 1<<4 (src/js/internal/streams/utils.ts:23,27). `Number(kDestroyed !== 0)` === 1. Instrumented run: `new Writable({objectMode:true, write(c,e,cb){cb()}})` + one `.write(obj)` per setImmediate turn → 5000 writes schedule 5000 `process.nextTick(afterWrite)` calls; with objectMode:false it schedules 0. Benchmark of 50k such writes in Bun: objectMode=true 102ms vs objectMode=false 80ms (≈27% overhead attributable solely to the extra nextTick+afterWrite per chunk). Node upstream still has the same bug (`(state[kState] & kDestroyed !== 0)`), so fixing it would make Bun faster than Node here.
findings.proposed_fix: Change line 618 to `const needTick = needDrain || (state[kState] & kDestroyed) !== 0 || cb !== nop;`.
findings.user_visible_impact: objectMode Transform/PassThrough streams (and any objectMode Writable with a sync _write) pay one extra process.nextTick + afterWrite invocation per chunk when chunks arrive one-per-tick (the normal pipe() pattern). Measured ≈27% extra wall-clock per chunk on a sync objectMode Writable; fixing it eliminates ~N redundant microtask schedulings for an N-chunk objectMode pipeline.

findings.title: 64 KB stack buffer zeroed on every POSIX Bun.file() read
findings.problem: `do_read_loop` declares `let mut stack_buffer = [0u8; 64 * 1024];`, which memsets 64 KB on every file read regardless of file size. The original Zig used `var stack_buffer: [64 * 1024]u8 = undefined;` (no memset). The in-code comment claims the memset is "negligible next to the per-iteration syscall", but for a small file the total syscall cost (open+fstat+read+read+close) is ~5-10 µs while a 64 KB memset is ~0.5-1 µs — a fixed 5-15% tax on every `Bun.file(path).text()/.bytes()/.arrayBuffer()`.
findings.evidence: read_file.rs:809 `let mut stack_buffer = [0u8; 64 * 1024];` vs. the pre-port Zig at `git show d4514457e8^:src/runtime/webcore/blob/read_file.zig` line 430: `var stack_buffer: [64 * 1024]u8 = undefined;`. `do_read` (line 510) already takes a raw `(*mut u8, usize)` pair, so the buffer could be `[MaybeUninit<u8>; 64*1024]` and passed as `as_mut_ptr().cast()` without ever materialising a `&[u8]` over uninit bytes (the stated UB concern); `bun_sys::read` would then need a `read_uninit` variant, which `node_fs.rs` already side-steps via `expand_to_capacity` on a fresh Vec.
findings.proposed_fix: Use `let mut stack_buffer = [MaybeUninit::<u8>::uninit(); 64 * 1024];` and pass `stack_buffer.as_mut_ptr().cast::<u8>()` to `remaining_buffer`/`do_read` (both already operate on raw `(*mut u8, usize)`). After `do_read` returns `read_amount`, only `stack_buffer[..read_amount]` is observed, which is initialized. Alternatively, skip the stack buffer entirely for `!self.could_block` (regular files) — see the companion finding on `remaining_buffer`.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every `await Bun.file(path).text()` / `.bytes()` / `.arrayBuffer()` on Linux/macOS pays a fixed ~0.5-1 µs 64 KB-zeroing cost. For small-file-heavy workloads (reading 10k config/source files), this is ~5-10 ms of pure memset that Node.js and the pre-Rust Bun did not pay.

findings.title: Files smaller than ~64 KB are read into the stack buffer then memcpy'd to the already-allocated heap buffer
findings.problem: `remaining_buffer` chooses the 64 KB stack buffer whenever `spare.len() < stack_buffer.len()`, i.e. whenever the heap buffer's spare capacity is < 64 KB. But for a regular file of size N, `run_async_with_fd` has just called `try_reserve_exact(N+16)` so spare is exactly N+16 — for any N < 65520, `spare < 64 KB` and the read goes into the stack buffer. `do_read_loop` then `extend_from_slice`'s the bytes into the heap buffer that was sitting ready. The pre-allocation at line 749-763 is defeated for the common case.
findings.evidence: read_file.rs:495 `if spare.len() < stack_buffer.len() { (stack_buffer.as_mut_ptr(), stack_buffer.len()) } else { (spare...) }` combined with read_file.rs:749-752 `try_reserve_exact(self.size + 16)` and read_file.rs:825-838 `if buf_ptr == stack_ptr { ... self.buffer.extend_from_slice(read); }`. For a 50 KB file: spare=51216 < 65536 → read 50 KB into stack → memcpy 50 KB into the 51 216-byte heap buffer that was allocated specifically to receive it.
findings.proposed_fix: Change the selection to prefer spare capacity whenever it is non-empty (or whenever `!self.could_block`): `if spare.is_empty() { stack } else { spare }`. For regular files, fstat gave us the size and we allocated exactly for it; reading directly into spare is correct and yields the same 2-syscall pattern (read N, read 0/EOF) without the N-byte copy. The stack buffer remains useful only for pipes/sockets (`could_block`) and the rare grew-after-fstat case.
findings.user_visible_impact: `Bun.file(path).bytes()` / `.arrayBuffer()` / `.text()` on any file under ~64 KB does one redundant memcpy of the entire file contents on Linux/macOS. For a 50 KB file that's ~50 KB of extra memory traffic per read; reading many source/config files is measurably slower than it needs to be (and slower than `fs.readFile`, which reads directly into its target buffer).

findings.title: Large ArrayBuffer input is fully memcpy'd into a new allocation before being written
findings.problem: When Bun.write(dest, data) receives an ArrayBuffer/TypedArray ≥ 256 KiB, the sync fast path is skipped and `Blob::get::<false,false>` (Blob.rs:5331) is invoked to turn `data` into a source Blob. For TypedArrays this hits `Blob::try_create(top_value.as_array_buffer(..).byte_slice(), ..)` (Blob.rs:3351) which lands on `bytes_.to_vec()` at Blob.rs:2544 — a full heap allocation + memcpy of the entire payload (or a memfd-backed copy above 8 MiB on Linux). The copied buffer is then passed to the WriteFile threadpool task, written to disk, and freed. The original JS-owned buffer is never written from directly.
findings.evidence: Call chain: write_file_internal (Blob.rs:5331 `break 'brk Blob::get::<false,false>(global_this, data)?`) → from_js_without_defer_gc (Blob.rs:3351 `Blob::try_create(top_value.as_array_buffer(global).unwrap().byte_slice(), global, false)`) → try_create (Blob.rs:2544 `Self::create_with_bytes_and_allocator(bytes_.to_vec(), global_this, was_string)`). Node's fs.promises.writeFile keeps a reference to the user's Buffer and writes it in place without copying.
findings.proposed_fix: For the Bun.write code path, construct a source blob that borrows the ArrayBuffer backing store instead of cloning it: hold a `jsc::Strong` on the ArrayBuffer value inside the WriteFile/WriteFileWindows task and point the bytes slice at `buffer_view.byte_slice()` (store::Bytes can already wrap an external allocator/owner). This removes one O(n) allocation + memcpy per large write.
findings.user_visible_impact: `await Bun.write(path, uint8array)` on a 1 GB Uint8Array spends ~1 GB extra RSS and a full 1 GB memcpy before the first write() syscall; throughput for large-buffer writes is roughly halved versus writing the buffer directly (Node does not copy).

findings.title: Inherited envp rebuilt from scratch on every Bun.spawn()
findings.problem: When the user does not pass `env` (the common case), `spawn_maybe_sync` calls `create_null_delimited_env_map()` on the VM's DotEnv `Map` every single time. That function (src/dotenv/env_loader.rs:1350) iterates the entire env HashMap and heap-allocates one `Box<[u8]>` per entry plus two Vecs. Nothing is cached, so 80–150 identical `K=V\0` strings are allocated, written and freed on every spawn even though the parent environment is unchanged between calls.
findings.evidence: js_bun_spawn_bindings.rs:914-932: `let envmap = ... create_null_delimited_env_map() ...; env_array.extend_from_slice(...)`. env_loader.rs:1350-1373: `let mut storage: Vec<Box<[u8]>> = Vec::with_capacity(envp_count); ... while let Some(pair) = it.next() { let mut env_buf = vec![0u8; klen + vlen + 2].into_boxed_slice(); ... }` — no cache field is consulted; a fresh NullDelimitedEnvMap is constructed each call.
findings.proposed_fix: Cache the `NullDelimitedEnvMap` on `dotenv::Map` (build it lazily on first spawn, invalidate when `put/remove` mutates the map). `spawn_maybe_sync` can then borrow the cached envp directly and only clone-and-append when IPC needs `NODE_CHANNEL_*` entries.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every `Bun.spawn({cmd})` / `Bun.spawnSync({cmd})` without an `env` option performs ~N+2 heap allocations (N = number of environment variables, typically 80–150) before the child is even forked. In a spawn-heavy workload (build scripts, test runners spawning per-file) this is the dominant pre-fork CPU/alloc cost and is entirely redundant across calls.

findings.title: Fresh Box<PathBuffer> allocated per spawn instead of using the pool, even when unused
findings.problem: `get_argv0` does `let mut path_buf: Box<bun_core::PathBuffer> = Box::default();` unconditionally. This heap-allocates 4 KB on Linux/1 KB on macOS/98 KB on Windows on every spawn and frees it at scope end. The buffer is only read inside the `else` branch that calls `which_for_spawn` — when the command contains `/` (absolute or relative path), `path_to_use` is empty and `path_buf` is never touched. The codebase already provides `bun_paths::path_buffer_pool::get()` (thread-local reuse, RAII return) specifically to avoid this.
findings.evidence: Line 173: `let mut path_buf: Box<bun_core::PathBuffer> = Box::default();` is placed before the `if path_to_use.is_empty()` check at line 194, so it runs on every call. src/paths/path_buffer_pool.rs shows the existing pool API (`path_buffer_pool::get()` -> `PoolGuard<PathBuffer>`). CLAUDE.md for src/ explicitly documents: "Use the path-buffer pool to avoid 64 KB stack allocations on Windows".
findings.proposed_fix: Move the buffer acquisition into the `else` branch that actually calls `which_for_spawn`, and use `bun_paths::path_buffer_pool::get()` instead of `Box::default()` so the buffer is reused across spawns.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every `Bun.spawn()` on Windows allocates and frees a 98 KB heap block before forking even when spawning an absolute path like `C:\Program Files\node.exe`; on Linux it is a 4 KB alloc/free per spawn. Shows up as avoidable allocator traffic in tight spawn loops.

findings.title: Per-env-var buffer zero-filled then immediately overwritten
findings.problem: Inside `create_null_delimited_env_map`, each `K=V\0` buffer is built with `vec![0u8; klen + vlen + 2].into_boxed_slice()`, which zeroes the whole allocation, and the next three statements overwrite every byte except the final NUL. For N env vars that's N redundant `memset`s per spawn. Using `Vec::with_capacity` + `extend_from_slice`/`push(0)` (or `Box::new_uninit_slice`) writes each byte exactly once and still yields `len == cap` so `into_boxed_slice` does not realloc.
findings.evidence: env_loader.rs:1359-1363: `let mut env_buf = vec![0u8; klen + vlen + 2].into_boxed_slice(); env_buf[..klen].copy_from_slice(pair.key_ptr); env_buf[klen] = b'='; env_buf[klen + 1..klen + 1 + vlen].copy_from_slice(&pair.value_ptr.value);` — every index in `0..klen+vlen+1` is stored twice.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace with `let mut env_buf = Vec::with_capacity(klen + vlen + 2); env_buf.extend_from_slice(pair.key_ptr); env_buf.push(b'='); env_buf.extend_from_slice(&pair.value_ptr.value); env_buf.push(0); let env_buf = env_buf.into_boxed_slice();` — single write per byte, no memset.
findings.user_visible_impact: Each `Bun.spawn()` that inherits the parent env pays one extra memset per environment variable (sum of all K=V lengths, commonly several KB) before forking. Compounds with the N+2 allocations from the first finding.

findings.title: on_message wraps the user callback in a redundant uws_ws_cork round-trip
findings.problem: Every inbound WebSocket message builds a Corker and calls `ws.cork(&mut corker, Corker::run)`. But uWS has already corked the socket for the entire duration of parsing: `WebSocketContext::onData` calls `asyncSocket->cork()` at line 330 before `consume()` (which is the only caller of `messageHandler`) and `asyncSocket->uncork()` at line 336. So `WebSocket::cork()` (WebSocket.h:275) always finds `isCorked()==true` and just invokes the handler. Reaching that no-op costs, per message: one Rust→C FFI call into `uws_ws_cork`, construction of a `MoveOnlyFunction<void()>` (SBO vtable setup), a `LoopData::findCorkSlot()` pointer scan, and a C→Rust FFI callback through the `extern "C" wrap<Corker>` thunk plus an indirect fn-pointer call to `Corker::run` — all before the real `on_message_handler.call(...)` runs.
findings.evidence: ServerWebSocket.rs:484 `ws.cork(&mut corker, Corker::run);`  →  uws_sys/WebSocket.rs:316-333 `uws_ws_cork(ssl, ws, Some(wrap::<C>), ud)`  →  libuwsockets.cpp:983 `uws->cork([handler,user_data](){ handler(user_data); })`  →  packages/bun-uws/src/WebSocket.h:275-288 `if (!Super::isCorked()) { ... } else { handler(); }`. The outer cork: packages/bun-uws/src/WebSocketContext.h:329-336 `asyncSocket->cork(); WebSocketProtocol<...>::consume(...); asyncSocket->uncork();` — `messageHandler` is only reached from inside `consume()` (lines 132, 193).
findings.proposed_fix: In `ServerWebSocket::on_message`, skip the FFI cork wrapper and invoke the callback directly, e.g. `let result = match on_message_handler.call(global_object, JSValue::UNDEFINED, &arguments) { Ok(v) => v, Err(e) => global_object.take_exception(e) };` (or `corker.run()` without `ws.cork`). The existing `onData` cork/uncork already batches any `ws.send()` calls made by the user handler. `on_open`/`on_drain` can keep their cork since those are not guaranteed to be inside an already-corked scope.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every `websocket.message(ws, msg)` dispatch pays two extra FFI crossings, an indirect MoveOnlyFunction call, and a cork-slot lookup that do nothing. In a tight echo benchmark (e.g. autocannon/k6 WS echo of small frames) this is fixed per-message overhead on the hot path; removing it raises messages/sec with no behavior change.

findings.title: send_inline_frame zero-initializes a 1 KB stack buffer on every small ws.send()
findings.problem: Every ws.send() of a payload <~1016 bytes (the overwhelming common case) goes through send_inline_frame, which declares `let mut inline_buf = [0u8; STACK_FRAME_SIZE];` (STACK_FRAME_SIZE = 1024). Rust emits a full 1024-byte memset, but `bytes.copy(...)` immediately overwrites exactly `inline_buf[..frame_size]` and only `&inline_buf[..frame_size]` is ever read (passed to enqueue_encoded_bytes → socket.write). The tail `[frame_size..1024]` is zeroed for nothing. LLVM cannot elide the memset because `copy()` writes the payload through an extern "C" highway_fill_with_skip_mask call whose write extent is opaque.
findings.evidence: Line 1339: `let mut inline_buf = [0u8; STACK_FRAME_SIZE];` followed by `bytes.copy(&self.global_this, &mut inline_buf[..frame_size], content_len, opcode);` and `self.enqueue_encoded_bytes(&inline_buf[..frame_size])`. For a 50-byte JSON string send, frame_size≈58, so ~966 bytes are memset-zeroed and never touched. Every write_string/write_binary_data/write_blob of <~1016 bytes hits this (see send_frame line 1297 and write_string line 1422/1429).
findings.proposed_fix: Use an uninitialized stack buffer, e.g. `let mut inline_buf = core::mem::MaybeUninit::<[u8; STACK_FRAME_SIZE]>::uninit();` and obtain `&mut [u8]` via `slice::from_raw_parts_mut(inline_buf.as_mut_ptr().cast::<u8>(), frame_size)`. `Copy::copy()` fully initializes `[..frame_size]` (header via write_header/split_frame, 4-byte mask via Mask::fill*, payload via copy_*_into_utf8/fill_with_skip_mask), so reading that range afterwards is sound.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every ws.send(smallString | smallBuffer) pays an extra ~1 KB memset. For a benchmark that calls ws.send() 1,000,000 times with 64-byte payloads, this is ~1 GB of redundant zero-writes — several percent of total send-path CPU on a fast loopback benchmark.

findings.title: With permessage-deflate negotiated, sub-860-byte string sends allocate+transcode to a throwaway Vec before deciding not to compress
findings.problem: `send_data()` (reached for any string send that misses the stack fast path: backpressure, or 16-bit strings ≥256 code units, or Latin-1 ≥~1 KB) first checks `may_compress` (deflate present + Text/Binary) and, if true, eagerly transcodes the source to UTF-8 into a freshly allocated `Vec<u8>` (lines 966-989). Only then does it call `should_compress(content_to_compress.len(), opcode)`, which is simply `len >= MIN_COMPRESS_SIZE (860)`. If the transcoded length is <860, it drops the Vec and calls `send_data_uncompressed(bytes, …)`, which re-scans (`frame_and_content_len()`) and re-encodes the same string from scratch. So every deflate-enabled string send that ends up below the compress threshold pays one heap alloc + one full UTF-8 transcode that is immediately discarded.
findings.evidence: Line 956-961 sets `may_compress` without looking at payload size. Lines 966-989 allocate `vec![0u8; content_byte_len]` and run `copy_utf16_into_utf8`/`copy_latin1_into_utf8`. Line 992: `if !self.should_compress(content_to_compress.len(), opcode) { return self.send_data_uncompressed(bytes, do_write, opcode); }`. `send_data_uncompressed` (line 1032) calls `bytes.frame_and_content_len()` (another full `element_length_*` scan) and `bytes.copy(…)` (another full transcode). The only size gate happens *after* the allocation.
findings.proposed_fix: Compute the UTF-8 byte length first via `element_length_latin1_into_utf8`/`element_length_utf16_into_utf8` (one SIMD pass, no allocation) and gate `should_compress` on that before allocating the Vec. Better: when compression is skipped, pass the already-transcoded buffer through as `Copy::Bytes(&utf8_storage)` instead of re-transcoding from `bytes` in `send_data_uncompressed`.
findings.user_visible_impact: On any WebSocket where the server negotiated permessage-deflate (common with uWS/most WS servers), every ws.send(string) that misses the inline fast path and whose UTF-8 length is <860 bytes does an extra heap allocation and an extra full-string transcode per call. A loop sending 500-byte JSON strings under backpressure (or 16-bit strings of 256-430 units) does 2× the transcoding work and one extra malloc/free per message vs. necessary.

findings.title: buf.indexOf(string) allocates a throwaway JSUint8Array for the needle on every call
findings.problem: indexOfString() encodes the needle string by calling constructFromEncoding(), which produces a full GC-managed JSC::JSUint8Array (Rust Vec<u8> → ArrayBuffer::createFromBytes + SharedTask deallocator → JSUint8Array::create with Buffer subclass structure). Only the raw byte pointer/length are ever read from it (arrayValue->byteLength() / ->typedVector()); the JS object, ArrayBuffer, and SharedTask are pure overhead and become garbage immediately. Node.js writes the needle into a plain byte buffer (StringBytes::Write) and searches that directly with no JS objects created.
findings.evidence: Lines 1683-1702: `JSC::EncodedJSValue encodedBuffer = constructFromEncoding(lexicalGlobalObject, view, encoding); ... auto* arrayValue = uncheckedDowncast<JSC::JSUint8Array>(JSC::JSValue::decode(encodedBuffer)); size_t needleLength = arrayValue->byteLength(); ... const uint8_t* typedVectorValue = arrayValue->typedVector();`. constructFromEncoding → Bun__encoding__constructFromLatin1 (src/runtime/webcore/encoding.rs:173) → JSValue::create_buffer → JSBuffer__bufferFromPointerAndLengthAndDeinit (JSBuffer.cpp:379) → ArrayBuffer::createFromBytes + JSUint8Array::create. Bench on release build: for a 1 KB haystack / 1-byte needle, buf.indexOf("x") = 243 ms vs buf.indexOf(Buffer.from("x")) = 33 ms for 500k iters (7.3x). Node.js same test: 73 ms vs 44 ms (1.65x).
findings.proposed_fix: Encode the needle into a plain heap/stack byte buffer instead of a JSUint8Array. Add a helper (e.g. a Rust FFI `Bun__encoding__encodeIntoVec` or reuse the existing Bun__encoding__writeLatin1/writeUTF16 into a small WTF::Vector<uint8_t, 64>) that returns pointer+length without constructing any JSC objects, then pass that to indexOf/lastIndexOf. As an additional fast path, when encoding==utf8/latin1/ascii and view.is8Bit() and (encoding!=utf8 || view is all-ASCII), search view.span8() directly with zero allocation.
findings.user_visible_impact: buf.indexOf("str") / buf.includes("str") / buf.lastIndexOf("str") are ~7x slower than buf.indexOf(Buffer) in Bun and ~3.3x slower than Node.js for small needles; every call creates ~4 heap allocations and one GC cell that could be zero.

findings.title: _callPendingCallbacks replaces an empty array with a fresh empty array on every end()/write()
findings.problem: `_callPendingCallbacks` is called unconditionally from `ServerResponse.prototype.end` (line 3226) and from the success path of `ServerResponse.prototype.write` (line 3369). When `this[kPendingCallbacks].length === 0` (the overwhelmingly common case — it is only non-empty after a backpressured write with a callback), the function still executes `this[kPendingCallbacks] = [];`, allocating a new empty Array to replace the existing empty Array. Combined with the constructor's `this[kPendingCallbacks] = []` (line 2154), a minimal `res.end("hello")` allocates two empty Arrays, and `res.write(a); res.write(b); res.end(c)` allocates four.
findings.proposed_fix: Add `if (originalLength === 0) return;` as the first line of `_callPendingCallbacks`. This skips the loop and the reassignment, leaving the existing empty array in place. (Optionally also make the constructor slot lazy — initialize to `null` and allocate on first push at line 3364 — to remove the remaining per-request array.)
findings.user_visible_impact: Every node:http request handled by `createServer((req,res)=>res.end(...))` allocates 1 extra empty Array per write/end that is immediately garbage. On a hello-world keep-alive benchmark this is one of the ~dozen per-request GC allocations and shows up as reduced req/s under load.

findings.title: res.end() allocates a fresh function object per request for the finish/close nextTick
findings.problem: `ServerResponse.prototype.end` finishes with `process.nextTick(function (self) { self.emit("finish"); process.nextTick(emitCloseNT, self); }, this);` (and a sibling `function (callback, self) {...}` in the callback branch at line 3230). Both are inline function expressions that close over nothing — their only free variables are the parameters passed via nextTick — yet a new Function object is created on every `end()` call. Every other per-request listener in this file was carefully hoisted to a shared module-level function (e.g. emitResponseFinishHandleSocket, emitAsyncResponseFinish, markResponseEndedNT) for exactly this reason; these two were missed.
findings.proposed_fix: Hoist both to module-level named functions, e.g. `function emitFinishThenCloseNT(self){ self.emit("finish"); process.nextTick(emitCloseNT, self); }` and `function emitFinishWithCallbackNT(callback, self){ ... }`, and pass those to `process.nextTick` instead. Zero behavior change.
findings.user_visible_impact: One avoidable Function allocation on every `res.end()` in node:http server. Measurable as extra GC pressure / lower throughput on high-RPS keep-alive benchmarks where `res.end()` is called once per request.

findings.title: Redundant Alias::get hashmap lookup in _resolve
findings.problem: `_resolve()` calls `Alias::get(specifier, Target::Bun, AliasCfg::default())` on line 4882, but its sole caller `resolve_hook()` already performed the identical lookup on line 5125 and returned early if it matched. `_resolve()` is only entered when the alias lookup has already missed, so this second lookup into `BUN_ALIAS_MAP` (a runtime `bun_collections::HashMap` with ~165 entries) is guaranteed to miss and is pure overhead: it re-hashes the full specifier bytes and probes the table again for every non-builtin `require()`/`import()` resolution — i.e. every filesystem module.
findings.evidence: jsc_hooks.rs:5125 in resolve_hook: `if let Some(hardcoded) = Alias::get(specifier_utf8.slice(), Target::Bun, AliasCfg::default()) { ... return true; }` — then at 5179 calls `_resolve(vm, specifier_utf8.slice(), ...)`. jsc_hooks.rs:4882 in _resolve: `if let Some(alias) = Alias::get(specifier, Target::Bun, AliasCfg::default()) { *ret_path = alias.path.as_bytes(); return Ok(()); }`. `rg "\b_resolve\(" src/runtime/jsc_hooks.rs` shows line 5179 is the only call site. `Alias::get` (HardcodedModule.rs:884) dispatches to `lookup(&BUN_ALIAS_MAP, name)` which is a `HashMap::get` on a LazyLock'd map — it hashes the full key each call, unlike the length-gated `comptime_string_map`.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete the `Alias::get` block at jsc_hooks.rs:4882-4885 (and, optionally, the always-false `bun:wrap`/`bun:main` checks above it whose bare-specifier form is also covered by resolve_hook's Alias::get). Alternatively thread an `already_checked_alias: bool` if additional call sites are anticipated.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every `require('./foo')` / `import('./foo')` that resolves to a filesystem path (the overwhelming common case) pays an extra full-path hash + hashmap probe. For a typical 50-80 byte absolute path this is ~100ns of wasted work per require; in a cold-start that loads thousands of CJS modules (e.g. `bun --bun eslint .`, next dev) it adds measurable overhead to module resolution.

findings.title: Two heap-allocated StringSourceProvider objects created per CommonJS module evaluation
findings.problem: `evaluateCommonJSModuleOnce`'s `initializeModuleObject` lambda calls `makeSource("resolve"_s, SourceOrigin(), ...)` and `makeSource("require"_s, SourceOrigin(), ...)` on every module load. `makeSource` does `adoptRef(*new StringSourceProvider(...))` — a fresh ThreadSafeRefCounted heap object each time — just to wrap the constant 7-byte strings "resolve" and "require" so `JSBoundFunction::create` can store a SourceCode. These SourceCode objects are identical for every module and should be cached once on ZigGlobalObject, not re-allocated per module. The same pattern repeats at lines 1658/1667 in the createRequire path.
findings.evidence: JSCommonJSModule.cpp:128 `SourceCode resolveSourceCode = makeSource("resolve"_s, SourceOrigin(), SourceTaintedOrigin::Untainted);` and :136 `SourceCode requireSourceCode = makeSource("require"_s, ...);` inside `initializeModuleObject`, which is called unconditionally at :204 for every CJS module. vendor/WebKit/Source/JavaScriptCore/parser/SourceCode.h:91 `inline SourceCode makeSource(...) { return SourceCode(StringSourceProvider::create(source, ...)); }` and SourceProvider.h:152 `return adoptRef(*new StringSourceProvider(...))`.
findings.proposed_fix: Add two cached `SourceCode` members to ZigGlobalObject (e.g. `m_requireBoundSourceCode` / `m_resolveBoundSourceCode`) initialized once, and reuse them in `initializeModuleObject` and the line 1658/1667 createRequire path. `SourceCode` is a RefPtr wrapper so reusing the same provider is safe.
findings.user_visible_impact: Loading N CommonJS modules performs 2N extra `new`/`delete` of ~100-byte `StringSourceProvider` objects on the JS heap allocator. For an app that requires 3000 CJS modules at startup (typical medium Node app) that is 6000 avoidable heap allocations during the hot module-load phase, contributing to GC pressure and slower cold start.

findings.title: .run() rebuilds column-name metadata it never reads, heap-allocating a PropertyNameArrayBuilder per call
findings.problem: `jsSQLStatementExecuteStatementFunctionRun` calls `initializeColumnNames()` after stepping, but `.run()` never constructs result objects and never reads `columnNames`/`_structure`. Because every write statement also bumps `version_db->version` just above (see first finding), `need_update()` is always true for writes, so `initializeColumnNames` runs on every `.run()` and (since `hasExecuted` is already true after the first call) does `castedThis->columnNames.reset(new PropertyNameArrayBuilder(...))` — one heap alloc + one heap free per `.run()` for data that is never consumed. The `columns` custom getter at line 2635 already self-initializes lazily, so this eager call is unnecessary.
findings.proposed_fix: Delete the `initializeColumnNames` block from `jsSQLStatementExecuteStatementFunctionRun` (and set `hasExecuted = true` directly if needed). The column-name getter already lazily initializes.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every `stmt.run(...)` on an INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE does one unnecessary operator-new + operator-delete of a PropertyNameArrayBuilder plus clears/rewrites of _structure/_prototype/validColumns. In an insert-heavy loop this is pure per-call overhead with zero benefit.

findings.title: FFIBuilder adds an extra JS closure call layer on every wrapped FFI invocation
findings.problem: For any dlopen/linkSymbols/CFunction symbol with >=1 arg, FFIBuilder creates `func = new Function('functionToCall', ...params, code)` and then wraps it again in a per-arity arrow: `wrap = (a,b) => func(functionToCall, a, b)`. The only purpose of `wrap` is to partially-apply `functionToCall`. Every user call therefore goes through two JS call frames (wrap → func) before reaching the native trampoline, and `functionToCall` is re-passed as an argument on every invocation instead of being closed over once.
findings.evidence: ffi.ts:361 `var func = new Function("functionToCall", ...paramNames, code);` followed by ffi.ts:370-408 which mints `wrap = (arg1, arg2) => func(functionToCall, arg1, arg2);` etc. and returns `wrap`. The native function is `functionToCall`; the returned callable is two hops away from it.
findings.proposed_fix: Generate the coercing function as a factory that captures functionToCall in a closure scope and return it directly: `var wrap = new Function('functionToCall', `return function ${JSON.stringify(name)}(${paramNames.join(',')}){${code}}`)(functionToCall);`. This yields one JS call per FFI invocation instead of two, removes the per-arity switch entirely, and still lets `Object.defineProperty` set the name. Alternatively use `func.bind(undefined, functionToCall)` so JSC's BoundFunctionCallIntrinsic applies.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every dlopen()'d symbol call with arguments pays for an extra JS function call. In LLInt/Baseline tiers this roughly doubles the JS-side overhead per FFI call; in a microbenchmark calling a trivial `int add(int,int)` the wrapper dominates over the native work.

findings.title: int64_t/uint64_t arg wrappers force a BigInt heap allocation and the _SLOW extern path for plain numbers
findings.problem: ffiWrappers[FFIType.int64_t] does `if (typeof val === 'number') return BigInt(val || 0);` and uint64_t does `return val <= 0 ? BigInt(0) : BigInt(val || 0);`. So passing `42` to an `args:['i64']` function allocates a JSBigInt cell on every call. Downstream, the TinyCC trampoline's JSVALUE_TO_INT64 (FFI.h:339-349) has inline fast paths for Int32-tagged and double-tagged JSValues and only falls back to the extern `JSVALUE_TO_INT64_SLOW` (JSC__JSValue__toInt64) for cells. By converting numbers to BigInt, the wrapper defeats the inline fast path and forces the out-of-line C++ call in addition to the allocation. Converting a double to BigInt gains no precision (precision was already lost when the literal became a double).
findings.evidence: ffi.ts:220-242 int64_t/uint64_t wrappers; FFI.h:339 `static int64_t JSVALUE_TO_INT64(EncodedJSValue value) { if (JSVALUE_IS_INT32(value)) return (int64_t)JSVALUE_TO_INT32(value); if (JSVALUE_IS_NUMBER(value)) return (int64_t)JSVALUE_TO_DOUBLE(value); return JSVALUE_TO_INT64_SLOW(value); }`; ffi_body.rs:2713 binds JSVALUE_TO_INT64_SLOW to JSC__JSValue__toInt64 (extern C++ in bindings.cpp:4018).
findings.proposed_fix: Change the int64_t/uint64_t wrappers to pass numbers through unchanged (e.g. `if (typeof val === 'number') return val || 0;`) and only keep BigInt for BigInt inputs, mirroring the i64_fast wrapper. The TCC stub already converts Int32/double JSValues to int64_t inline with no allocation or extern call.
findings.user_visible_impact: Calling an FFI function declared with `args:['i64']` or `['u64']` and passing a JS number allocates one BigInt per argument per call and takes an extra out-of-line C++ call; for a 1-arg int64 function this is the difference between ~0 extra allocations and 1 GC cell per call.

findings.title: i64_fast/u64_fast wrappers re-evaluate BigInt(Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER) on every bigint arg
findings.problem: The i64_fast wrapper body contains `if (val <= BigInt(Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER) && val >= BigInt(-Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER))` and the u64_fast body `val <= BigInt(Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER) && val >= BigInt(0)`. These bodies are inlined as source text into the per-symbol `new Function(...)` trampoline, so `BigInt(Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER)` is re-evaluated on every call that passes a bigint — a Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER property get plus a BigInt allocation, twice. (The i64_fast block is also pasted twice at lines 185-206; the second assignment is dead.)
findings.evidence: ffi.ts:196-206 and 244-251; these strings become the body of `(val=>{...})(pN)` at ffi.ts:346 and are executed per call. `BigInt(Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER)` is not a literal so JSC cannot constant-fold it without proving `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` and global `BigInt` are untouched.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace with BigInt literals that JSC parses once: `if (val <= 9007199254740991n && val >= -9007199254740991n)` and `val >= 0n`. Remove the duplicated i64_fast assignment at lines 185-195.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every call to an i64_fast/u64_fast FFI function with a bigint argument does 2 avoidable BigInt allocations and a global property lookup per such argument.

findings.title: NativeReadable zero-fills its 256KB–2MB read buffer with Buffer.alloc before every native pull()
findings.problem: `getRemainingChunk` allocates the pull buffer with `Buffer.alloc(size)` (zero-filled) where `size` defaults to `stream[kHighWaterMark]` = 256KB and can grow to 2MB via `adjustHighWaterMark`. The buffer is immediately handed to the native `ptr.pull(chunk, ...)` which overwrites the first `result` bytes; only `chunk.subarray(0, result)` is ever pushed to the user and the remainder stays internal as `kRemainingChunk`. The memset done by `Buffer.alloc` is therefore pure waste. A new allocation happens whenever the previous chunk was fully consumed (`result === chunk.byteLength`) or the remainder is <512 bytes — i.e. on essentially every read of a high-throughput source.
findings.evidence: Line 117: `stream[kRemainingChunk] = chunk = Buffer.alloc(size);` with default `size` 256*1024 (line 76). In Bun, `Buffer.alloc(262144)` measured ~34µs vs `Buffer.allocUnsafe(262144)` ~2µs (16× slower, 342ms vs 21ms over 10k iterations). Safety: `handleNumberResult` pushes only `chunk.subarray(0, result)` (line 199) and keeps `chunk.subarray(result)` internal (line 200); `handleArrayBufferViewResult` pushes the native-returned view, not `chunk`. No path exposes uninitialized bytes, so `allocUnsafe` is safe here.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace `Buffer.alloc(size)` with `Buffer.allocUnsafe(size)` at line 117.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every chunk read from a Bun NativeReadable (child_process stdout/stderr, Readable.fromWeb over a native-backed ReadableStream, etc.) pays ~30µs of memset for a 256KB buffer (and proportionally more as the buffer auto-grows to 2MB). For a subprocess producing 1GB of output in 256KB chunks, that is ~4096 unnecessary 256KB memsets ≈ 130ms of pure zero-fill; switching to allocUnsafe removes it.

findings.title: Every `new URL()` inserts into a global DOMObjectWrapperMap + allocates a JSC::Weak handle that is never consulted
findings.problem: `toJSNewlyCreated(... DOMURL ...)` calls `createWrapper<DOMURL>` (JSDOMWrapperCache.h:183-192), which after allocating the JSDOMURL cell calls `cacheWrapper(world, domObjectPtr, wrapper)`. DOMURL does NOT inherit ScriptWrappable, so `setInlineCachedWrapper(DOMWrapperWorld&, void*, ...)` returns false and the code falls through to `weakAdd(world.wrappers(), wrapperKey(domObject), JSC::Weak<JSObject>(wrapper, owner, &world))` — a HashMap insert plus a WeakImpl block allocation on every `new URL()`. On GC, `JSDOMURLOwner::finalize` (line 818-823) runs `uncacheWrapper`, a HashMap remove. `isReachableFromOpaqueRoots` is hard-coded `return false` (line 815), and a grep shows nothing ever calls `toJS(..., DOMURL&)` with an existing DOMURL (the only callers pass freshly-allocated objects). The cache entry is created and destroyed without ever being read.
findings.evidence: JSDOMWrapperCache.h:168-174 `cacheWrapper` → `setInlineCachedWrapper(DOMWrapperWorld&, void*, ...)` (line 112) returns false → `weakAdd(world.wrappers(), ...)`. DOMURL.h:43 `class DOMURL final : public RefCounted<DOMURL>, public CanMakeWeakPtr<DOMURL>, public URLDecomposition` — no ScriptWrappable. JSDOMURL.cpp:810-816 `isReachableFromOpaqueRoots(...) { return false; }`. `grep toJS.*DOMURL` finds no lookup callers.
findings.proposed_fix: Bypass `cacheWrapper`/`uncacheWrapper` for DOMURL: have `toJSNewlyCreated(..., Ref<DOMURL>&&)` call `JSDOMURL::create(getDOMStructure<JSDOMURL>(vm, *globalObject), globalObject, WTF::move(impl))` directly (no `createWrapper`), drop `JSDOMURLOwner`/`finalize`, and make `toJS(..., DOMURL&)` an error or delegate to `toJSNewlyCreated`. Alternatively make DOMURL a `ScriptWrappable` so the inline wrapper slot is used instead of the global HashMap (still one Weak alloc but no HashMap churn).
findings.user_visible_impact: Every `new URL(str)` / `URL.parse(str)` pays for one WeakImpl allocation + one HashMap::set into DOMWrapperWorld::m_wrappers, and one HashMap::remove at GC; at ~1M URLs/sec this is measurable constructor overhead and adds per-URL work to full-GC sweeps.

findings.title: URL component setters serialize and re-parse the whole URL via `setFullURL` → `setHref`
findings.problem: `DOMURL::setFullURL(const URL& fullURL)` is implemented as `setHref(fullURL.string())`, and `setHref` (DOMURL.cpp:113-124) does `URL completeURL { URL{}, url }` — a full URLParser pass. Every `URLDecomposition` setter (setProtocol/setHost/setHostname/setPort/setPathname/setSearch/setHash, URLDecomposition.cpp:57-254) copies `m_url` via `fullURL()`, mutates that copy (which is already a valid parsed `WTF::URL`), then hands it to `setFullURL` which throws the parsed form away, serializes to a String, and runs URLParser again from scratch. The re-parse is entirely redundant — the `WTF::URL` being passed in is already valid.
findings.evidence: DOMURL.h:78 `void setFullURL(const URL& fullURL) final { setHref(fullURL.string()); }`. DOMURL.cpp:115 `URL completeURL { URL {}, url };` inside setHref. URLDecomposition.cpp e.g. 212-219 setPathname: `auto fullURL = this->fullURL(); ... fullURL.setPath(value); setFullURL(fullURL);`.
findings.proposed_fix: Override setFullURL to assign directly: `void setFullURL(const URL& fullURL) final { m_url = fullURL; if (m_searchParams) m_searchParams->updateFromAssociatedURL(); }` (or add a `DOMURL::setURL(URL&&)` and call that). This removes one full URLParser invocation per setter call.
findings.user_visible_impact: `url.pathname = '/api'`, `url.search = '?x=1'`, `url.hash = '#h'` etc. are roughly 2× slower than necessary because the entire URL string is re-parsed on every assignment; on long URLs (e.g. 1KB href) the redundant parse dominates.

findings.title: Every console.log heap-allocates a ScriptArguments + N+1 Strong GC handles that are immediately discarded
findings.problem: Bun uses JSC's stock `consoleProtoFuncLog` → `createScriptArguments()` path. That path heap-allocates a ref-counted `ScriptArguments`, heap-allocates a `Vector<Strong<Unknown>>`, acquires a `Strong` GC handle for each argument plus one for the global, and then hands it to Bun's `ConsoleObject::messageWithTypeAndLevel`. Bun then copies every argument back out into a 2 KB stack array (`JSC::EncodedJSValue jsArgs[255]`) and calls into Rust, after which all the Strong handles are released and both heap blocks freed. Because Bun prints synchronously while the CallFrame still roots the arguments, none of the `Strong` handles or the `ScriptArguments` allocation are needed.
findings.evidence: createScriptArguments (vendor/WebKit/Source/JavaScriptCore/inspector/ScriptCallStackFactory.cpp:220-227): `Vector<Strong<Unknown>> arguments; for (...) arguments.append({ vm, callFrame->uncheckedArgument(i) }); return ScriptArguments::create(...);` where `ScriptArguments::create` does `adoptRef(*new ScriptArguments(...))` and the ctor also does `m_globalObject(vm, globalObject)`. Then in /workspace/bun/src/jsc/bindings/ConsoleObject.cpp:44-58: `JSC::EncodedJSValue jsArgs[255]; for (...) jsArgs[i] = JSC::JSValue::encode(args->argumentAt(i)); Bun__ConsoleObject__messageWithTypeAndLevel(..., jsArgs, count);`.
findings.proposed_fix: Install Bun-native host functions for `console.log/info/debug/warn/error` (alongside the existing `consoleObject->putDirectBuiltinFunction` calls in ZigGlobalObject.cpp:3136-3140) that read `callFrame->argument(i)` into a small stack buffer and call `Bun__ConsoleObject__messageWithTypeAndLevel` directly. Only fall back to constructing a `ScriptArguments` when `globalObject->inspectable()` is true (inspector attached). This removes 2 heap allocations and N+1 HandleSet insert/remove per call.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every `console.log(...)` call does 2 heap allocations and N+1 JSC HandleSet operations before any formatting starts. In a tight logging loop (e.g. `for (let i=0;i<1e6;i++) console.log(i)` piped to /dev/null) this is a double-digit-percent share of total time and GC-handle-table churn; Node.js avoids it entirely by implementing console.log in JS.

findings.title: `Tag::get` re-atomizes the "$$typeof" Identifier for every object/array value formatted
findings.problem: For every value whose JSType satisfies `is_object()` (plain objects, arrays, maps, sets, iterators, dates, errors, …), `Tag::get_advanced` calls `value.get_own_truthy(global_this, "$$typeof")` to detect React elements. `get_own_truthy` goes through `JSC__JSValue__getOwn`, which on every call builds a WTF::String from the byte slice and then does `JSC::Identifier::fromString(vm, ...)` — an AtomString hash-table lookup. This per-value atomization is paid for every nested object in the log output, almost none of which are React elements.
findings.evidence: ConsoleObject.rs:2315-2316 `if js_type.is_object() && js_type != jsc::JSType::ProxyObject { if let Some(typeof_symbol) = value.get_own_truthy(global_this, "$$typeof")? { ... } }`. bindings.cpp:4244-4260 `JSC__JSValue__getOwn`: `WTF::String propertyNameString = propertyName->toWTFString(BunString::ZeroCopy); auto identifier = JSC::Identifier::fromString(vm, propertyNameString);` — runs per call. Contrast with the `InspectCustom` lookup one screen above (line 2249) which uses `fast_get(global_this, jsc::BuiltinName::InspectCustom)` with a pre-cached Identifier.
findings.proposed_fix: Add `$$typeof` to the `BuiltinName` table and use `value.fast_get_own(global, BuiltinName::DollarDollarTypeof)` (own-only variant) so the Identifier is allocated once per VM, not once per formatted value. Alternatively cache the Identifier on ZigGlobalObject and expose a dedicated FFI like the existing `fastGet`.
findings.user_visible_impact: `console.log(arrayOf1000Objects)` performs 1001 AtomString table lookups + 1001 WTF::String constructions just for JSX detection before printing a single byte; shows up as measurable overhead when logging large arrays/objects compared to Node.

findings.title: `print_string` heap-allocates for Latin-1→UTF-8 while an allocation-free streaming helper sits unused in the same file
findings.problem: For a JSString that is 8-bit Latin-1 with at least one byte ≥ 0x80 (accented characters, ©, ™, etc.), `print_string` does `let buf = strings::allocate_latin1_into_utf8(str.latin1()).unwrap_or_default(); writer.write_all(&buf);` — a fresh `Vec<u8>` allocation + copy per call. `WrappedWriter::write_latin1` (defined at line 2827 in the same module) already implements streaming Latin-1→UTF-8 with zero allocation by writing ASCII runs and 2-byte sequences directly to the buffered writer, but it is never called anywhere in ConsoleObject.rs — it is dead code that was apparently written for exactly this spot and never wired up.
findings.evidence: ConsoleObject.rs:3779-3784 `} else if !str.is_empty() { let buf = strings::allocate_latin1_into_utf8(str.latin1()).unwrap_or_default(); if !buf.is_empty() { writer.write_all(&buf); } }`. ConsoleObject.rs:2827 `pub fn write_latin1(&mut self, buf: &[u8])` — `grep -rn write_latin1 src/jsc/ConsoleObject.rs` returns only the definition line, no callers.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the `allocate_latin1_into_utf8` branch with `writer.write_latin1(str.latin1())`. This also lets the `as_utf8()` ASCII-scan be dropped in favor of a single `is_utf16()`/`else write_latin1` branch (write_latin1 already fast-paths pure-ASCII runs via `first_non_ascii`), avoiding the double scan on ASCII strings.
findings.user_visible_impact: `console.log(stringWithAccentedChars)` (e.g. i18n log lines containing é, ü, ñ stored as Latin-1 JSStrings) does an extra heap alloc+free per call proportional to string length; long localized log lines pay O(n) allocation every time instead of zero.

findings.title: PathLike is heap-cloned in `get_fd_by_opening` purely to satisfy borrowck
findings.problem: `get_fd_by_opening` does `let path_string = match self.pathlike() { Path(p) => p.clone(), ... }` before `slice_z`. `PathLike::clone()` on an owned `String(CowSlice)` calls `CowSlice::init_dupe` (heap alloc + memcpy of the path bytes). This is on top of the `store.data.as_file().clone()` in `ReadFile::create_with_ctx` (read_file.rs:346) which already cloned the same path once. The clone exists only because `self.pathlike()` borrows `&self` while `self.set_opened_fd(..)` later needs `&mut self`; `slice_z` copies into the stack `PathBuffer` anyway, so the borrow of `self` does not actually need to outlive that call.
findings.evidence: Blob.rs:7120-7124: `let path_string = match self.pathlike() { PathOrFileDescriptor::Path(p) => p.clone(), ... }; let path = path_string.slice_z(&mut buf);` and node_path.rs:112-116: `Self::String(s) => Self::String(if s.is_owned() { bun_core::handle_oom(CowSlice::init_dupe(s.slice())) } else { s.borrow() })`. `slice_z` (src/runtime/node/types.rs:1079-1082) ultimately `copy_from_slice`s into `buf` and returns a `ZStr` backed by `buf`, not by `self`.
findings.proposed_fix: Compute the NUL-terminated path into `buf` via a helper that takes `&[u8]` (e.g. copy `self.pathlike().path().slice()` into `buf` and construct a `ZStr` from `buf` in a narrow scope), so no live borrow of `self` survives to conflict with the later `&mut self` calls. Alternatively, have `FileOpener::pathlike()` return the path slice by value into the caller-provided `PathBuffer` directly. Either eliminates one heap alloc + memcpy of the path string per `Bun.file(path).{text,bytes,arrayBuffer}()` (and per `Bun.write`).
findings.user_visible_impact: Every path-backed `Bun.file(path).text()` / `.bytes()` / `.arrayBuffer()` on POSIX performs 1-2 extra small heap allocations and path-length memcpys before even calling open(). For a microbenchmark reading the same small file in a loop this shows up as allocator pressure that Node's `fs.readFile` (single path buffer, no clone) does not have.

findings.title: Async string path scans the entire UTF-8 payload with is_all_ascii() though Bun.write never reads the flag
findings.problem: For `Bun.write(dest, bigString)` where the string is ≥ 256 KiB, the source blob is built via `Blob::get::<false,false>` → string arm (Blob.rs:3330-3333): `let bytes = str.to_owned_slice(); let ascii = strings::is_all_ascii(&bytes); Blob::init_with_all_ascii(bytes, global, ascii)`. `is_all_ascii()` linearly scans the full byte buffer to set `Blob::charset`, but the WriteFile path (write_file_with_source_destination → WriteFile/WriteFileWindows) never consults `charset`/`is_all_ascii` — it only calls `shared_view()` and writes the raw bytes. The scan is dead work proportional to payload size on every large-string Bun.write.
findings.evidence: Blob.rs:3331-3333 computes `ascii`; write_file.rs and write_file_with_source_destination (Blob.rs:4747-4801) read only `source_blob.borrowed_view()` / `shared_view()` and never touch `charset` or `is_all_ascii`.
findings.proposed_fix: In write_file_internal, special-case `data.is_string()` on the async fallback to build the bytes blob without the ASCII scan (e.g. `Blob::create_with_bytes_and_allocator(str.to_owned_slice(), global, true)` directly, or add a `Blob::get` variant that skips the ascii classification). Alternatively, make the ascii flag lazy.
findings.user_visible_impact: `Bun.write(path, hundredMBString)` does an extra ~100 MB linear scan (≈5–10 ms on a modern CPU) on the main JS thread before scheduling the write, added to the already-required UTF-8 transcode.

findings.title: Inherited envp pointer array copied into a second Vec when no IPC is in use
findings.problem: `create_null_delimited_env_map()` already returns a NULL-terminated `Box<[*const c_char]>` that is a valid `envp`. The code then strips the trailing NULL, `extend_from_slice`s all N pointers into the separate (empty) `env_array: Vec<CStrPtr>`, and at line 1024 `push`es NULL back — one Vec allocation plus a likely realloc on the final push. The only reason for this round-trip is the rare IPC path that appends `NODE_CHANNEL_FD=` / `NODE_CHANNEL_SERIALIZATION_MODE=`; when `maybe_ipc_mode.is_none()` (the overwhelmingly common case) the copied array is byte-identical to `envmap.envp`.
findings.evidence: Lines 930-931: `let entries = envmap.as_slice(); env_array.extend_from_slice(&entries[..entries.len().saturating_sub(1)]);` followed by line 1024: `env_array.push(core::ptr::null());` and line 1180: `spawn::spawn_process(&spawn_options, argv.as_ptr(), env_array.as_ptr())`. `env_array` starts as `Vec::new()` (line 369) so `extend_from_slice` allocates and the `push` may realloc.
findings.proposed_fix: Fast-path: when `!override_env && maybe_ipc_mode.is_none()`, pass `envmap.as_ptr()` directly to `spawn_process` and skip populating `env_array` entirely. Only fall back to building a mutable Vec when IPC needs to append entries.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every `Bun.spawn()` without `env` and without `ipc` (the default call shape) does an extra Vec allocation + memcpy of N pointers + potential realloc that produces the exact same envp that was just built. Small per-call cost but on the hot path of every spawn.

findings.title: Inbound text frames are scanned for UTF-8/ASCII two to three times
findings.problem: For `Opcode::Text`, uWS has already validated the payload with `simdutf::validate_utf8` before calling `messageHandler` (WebSocketContext.h:125/186 → WebSocketProtocol.h:117-120). `on_message` then calls `create_utf8_for_js` → `BunString__createUTF8ForJS` (BunString.cpp:94-113), which re-scans the same bytes with `simdutf::validate_ascii` and, when any non-ASCII byte is present, runs `WTF::String::fromUTF8ReplacingInvalidSequences`, a validating decoder that walks the buffer a third time checking for invalid sequences that cannot exist. So ASCII text is scanned 2x, non-ASCII text 3x, per message.
findings.evidence: ServerWebSocket.rs:467 `Opcode::Text => jsc::bun_string_jsc::create_utf8_for_js(global_object, message)`; BunString.cpp:101-105 `if (simdutf::validate_ascii(ptr,length)) {...} auto str = WTF::String::fromUTF8ReplacingInvalidSequences(...)`; WebSocketContext.h:125 `if (opCode == 1 && !protocol::isValidUtf8((unsigned char *) data, length)) { forceClose(...); return true; }`; WebSocketProtocol.h:117-120 `static bool isValidUtf8(...) { return simdutf::validate_utf8(...); }`.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a trusted-UTF-8 JSString constructor (e.g. `BunString__createValidatedUTF8ForJS`) that either (a) uses simdutf's single-pass `utf8_length_from_utf8`/`convert_valid_utf8_to_utf16le` to pick 8-bit vs 16-bit without a separate validate pass, or (b) calls `WTF::String::fromUTF8` (non-replacing) instead of `fromUTF8ReplacingInvalidSequences` so the decoder does not carry replacement-logic overhead. Call it from `on_message` for the text branch.
findings.user_visible_impact: Receiving a 1 MB JSON text frame scans ~1 MB (utf8 validate in uWS) + ~1 MB (ascii validate) + ~1 MB (replacing decoder) = ~3 MB of SIMD scanning per message for non-ASCII payloads; ~2 MB for pure ASCII. Removing the redundant passes reduces CPU on message-heavy workloads with large text frames (chat, GraphQL subscriptions).

findings.title: All-ASCII string sends do two payload passes (Latin-1→UTF-8 copy, then mask-in-place) where one fused mask-copy suffices
findings.problem: `Copy::copy_with_compressed_flag` for `Copy::Latin1` does `copy_latin1_into_utf8(parts.payload, latin1)` (SIMD pass #1: copy-while-checking-for-high-bit) followed by `Mask::fill_in_place(global_this, parts.mask, parts.payload)` (SIMD pass #2: xor-in-place). For `Copy::Bytes` it instead does `Mask::fill(global_this, parts.mask, parts.payload, bytes)` — a single fused xor-copy pass. When the Latin-1 input is all-ASCII (UTF-8 bytes == source bytes), the two-pass path is semantically identical to the one-pass Bytes path but touches the payload memory twice. The caller already knows whether the input is all-ASCII: `write_string` line 1420 computed `byte_len = element_length_latin1_into_utf8(latin1)`, and `byte_len == latin1.len()` iff ASCII.
findings.evidence: Lines 2336-2344 (`Copy::Latin1` arm) vs lines 2346-2351 (`Copy::Bytes` arm). `write_string` line 1419-1422 constructs `Copy::Latin1(str.slice())` unconditionally for 8-bit JSC strings, even when `byte_len == str.slice().len()` proves it is ASCII. `copy_latin1_into_utf8` (bun_core/lib.rs:1834) → `copy_ascii_prefix` is a full SIMD copy pass; `Mask::fill_in_place` (line 2088) is a second full SIMD pass over the same bytes.
findings.proposed_fix: In `write_string` (and the Latin1 arm of `send_data`), after computing `(frame_size, byte_len) = Copy::Latin1(slice).frame_and_content_len()`, if `byte_len == slice.len()` switch to `Copy::Bytes(slice)` before calling `send_inline_frame`/`send_data_uncompressed`. That routes through the single-pass `Mask::fill` and also skips the redundant second high-bit scan inside `copy_latin1_into_utf8`.
findings.user_visible_impact: ws.send("ascii string") — the dominant case for JSON/text protocols — reads+writes the payload bytes twice instead of once during framing. For large ASCII text sends (e.g. 4 KB JSON via the send_data_uncompressed path) this roughly doubles the per-byte memory traffic of framing; for the <1 KB stack path it adds a second SIMD sweep over every payload byte.

findings.title: Every compressed inbound message allocates and frees a fresh 128 KB Vec
findings.problem: `dispatch_compressed_data()` calls `deflate.rare_data.array_list()` for the decompression output buffer. `RareData::array_list()` returns `Vec::with_capacity(128 * 1024)` — a fresh 128 KB heap allocation — and the Vec is dropped after `dispatch_data()`. There is no reuse across messages. The struct even has a comment acknowledging this (`// PERF: allocates a fresh heap Vec per call — profile if hot.`), and the field/type name `rare_data` suggests it was meant to cache a reusable buffer, but the caching was never implemented.
findings.evidence: WebSocketDeflate.rs:44-47: `pub fn array_list(&self) -> Vec<u8> { Vec::with_capacity(Self::STACK_BUFFER_SIZE) }` with `STACK_BUFFER_SIZE = 128 * 1024`. websocket_client.rs:383: `let mut decompressed = deflate.rare_data.array_list();` → `deflate.decompress(data, &mut decompressed)` → `self.dispatch_data(items, kind)` → `decompressed` dropped at end of scope. Runs once per RSV1-marked message.
findings.proposed_fix: Store the output `Vec<u8>` on `RareData` (or on `PerMessageDeflate`) and hand out `&mut Vec<u8>` after `.clear()` instead of constructing a new one, so the 128 KB capacity is reused across messages on the same connection. The borrow of `deflate` is already dropped before `dispatch_data` re-enters, so a `mem::take`/`replace` pattern (like `send_buffer_out` uses) avoids the aliasing concern.
findings.user_visible_impact: Receiving compressed WebSocket messages (permessage-deflate) incurs a 128 KB malloc + free per message regardless of decompressed size. At 10k small compressed messages/sec this is ~1.3 GB/s of allocator churn that a reused buffer would eliminate.

findings.title: indexOf16 / lastIndexOf16 use naive std::search instead of SIMD for UTF-16 encoding
findings.problem: The byte-wise indexOf() path (line 1564) uses SIMD-accelerated highway_memmem / highway_index_of_char, but the UTF-16 path indexOf16()/lastIndexOf16() (lines 1569-1598) falls back to std::search / std::find_end, which are the naive O(n*m) brute-force algorithms with no vectorization. There is no SIMD or Boyer-Moore style skipping.
findings.evidence: Line 1578: `auto it = std::search(haystack.begin(), haystack.end(), needle.begin(), needle.end());` vs line 1564: `void* result = highway_memmem(haystackPtr, haystackLen, valuePtr, static_cast<size_t>(valueLength));`. Bench on release build: 100 KB zero-filled buffer, 5k iters — utf8 buffer needle (SIMD) 15.3 ms, utf16le buffer needle (std::search) 56.0 ms for the same byte count.
findings.proposed_fix: Reinterpret the u16 span as bytes and call highway_memmem on it, then reject matches that land on odd byte offsets and continue (matches are rare compared to scan volume). Alternatively add a highway_memmem16 that searches on u16 code units, or at minimum use WTF::BoyerMooreHorspoolTable for multi-char needles.
findings.user_visible_impact: buf.indexOf(needle, 0, 'utf16le') and buf.indexOf(needle, 0, 'ucs2') on large buffers are ~3-4x slower than the equivalent utf8/latin1 search; degrades further toward O(n*m) when the haystack contains many partial-prefix matches.

findings.title: response.text() all-ASCII path shrinks the body Vec via into_boxed_slice() before handing it to JSC
findings.problem: Internal::to_string_owned (the InternalBlob → JS string path used by every `await (await fetch()).text()` once the body is buffered) converts the body `Vec<u8>` to a `Box<[u8]>` before passing it to `to_external_value`. `into_boxed_slice()` calls `shrink_to_fit()`, which issues a `realloc()` whenever `cap != len`. For fetch, the bytes come from `FetchTasklet.scheduled_response_buffer.list`, which is grown with `extend_from_slice` (geometric growth, see FetchTasklet.rs:2430 → MutableString.rs:449), so any response that arrives in more than one chunk has `cap > len`. The JS-side finalizer (`ZigString__freeGlobal` → `default_alloc::free`, ZigString.rs:127) only needs the pointer, not the length, so the shrink buys nothing.
findings.proposed_fix: Match the arrayBuffer path: `let owned = core::mem::ManuallyDrop::new(self.to_owned_slice()); let ptr = core::ptr::slice_from_raw_parts_mut(owned.as_ptr() as *mut u8, owned.len());` (or `Vec::leak()`), then feed that into `ZigString::init` + `to_external_value`. The external-string finalizer frees with `mi_free(ptr)` and ignores size, so no shrink is required.
findings.user_visible_impact: `await (await fetch(url)).text()` on an all-ASCII body that arrives in multiple chunks (any body larger than a single socket read, or gzip/chunked) pays one extra mimalloc `realloc()` on the full body; when `len < cap/2` (common right after a doubling) that is an N-byte memcpy of the whole response. `.arrayBuffer()` / `.bytes()` on the same response do not pay it.

findings.title: IncomingMessage allocates a fresh {highWaterMark} options object per request; could reuse a scratch object like ServerResponse does
findings.problem: On the native-server fast path, the IncomingMessage constructor does `Readable.$call(this, arguments[7] ? { highWaterMark: arguments[7].readableHighWaterMark } : undefined);`. `arguments[7]` (the socket) is always truthy on the server path, so every request allocates a fresh single-property options object. The Readable constructor consumes this object synchronously (internal/streams/readable.ts:96 reads `options.highWaterMark` once into ReadableState) and never retains it. The dispatcher already applies exactly this optimization for ServerResponse via the module-level `scratchResponseOptions` (src/js/node/_http_server.ts:811-817, 1391-1395, with the comment "The builtin ServerResponse consumes its options synchronously, so a reusable scratch object avoids one allocation per request").
findings.evidence:
Readable.$call(this, arguments[7] ? { highWaterMark: arguments[7].readableHighWaterMark } : undefined);
// vs. _http_server.ts line 811-817 which reuses `scratchResponseOptions` for the same reason.
findings.proposed_fix: Mirror the scratchResponseOptions pattern: a module-level `const scratchIncomingOptions = { highWaterMark: 0 }`; set `scratchIncomingOptions.highWaterMark = arguments[7].readableHighWaterMark` and pass it. Alternatively, cache the options object on the socket (it's constant per keep-alive connection) since many requests share the same socket.
findings.user_visible_impact: One object allocation eliminated per incoming node:http request. On a keep-alive `createServer` hello-world benchmark this removes one of the handful of per-request GC allocations, improving sustained req/s.

findings.title: Per-request onClose closure is allocated even for synchronously-finished responses that never use it
findings.problem: Inside `onNodeHTTPRequest`, `function onClose() { didFinish = true; if (resolveFunction) resolveFunction(); }` is a function declaration that captures `didFinish` and `resolveFunction`, so a closure is allocated on every request. It is installed via `setCloseCallback(http_res, onClose)` (line 960) before `emit("request")`. For the common benchmark case where the handler calls `res.end()` synchronously, `handle.finished` is already true when control returns from `emit`, the dispatcher takes the `if (handle.finished || didFinish)` branch at line 1072 and immediately clears the callback with `http_res[kCloseCallback] = undefined` (line 1074) without it ever running. The closure (and the `resolveFunction`/`didFinish` activation slots) are allocated and discarded per request. Note `res.end()` schedules finish/close via nextTick, so `didFinish` cannot be true at line 1072 on the sync path — only `handle.finished` matters there.
findings.proposed_fix: Move the closure/promise-capability creation to after the `handle.finished` early-return check. Concretely: do not declare `onClose`/`didFinish`/`resolveFunction` up front; after `emit("request")`, check `handle.finished` first and return early. Only if the response is still outstanding, create `{resolve, promise} = $newPromiseCapability(Promise)` and install a close callback that calls `resolve`. Since `res.end()` emits finish/close via nextTick (not synchronously during emit), no close callback is needed before the sync-finished check.
findings.user_visible_impact: For the canonical `createServer((req,res)=>res.end("ok"))` benchmark (and any handler that responds synchronously), one closure allocation per request is eliminated. This is the dominant request shape for static responses, JSON APIs with cached data, etc.

findings.title: `basename()` backward scan on every resolve for a bun:wrap case that Alias::get already handles
findings.problem: The first thing `_resolve` does for every specifier is `if bun_paths::basename(specifier) == b"bun:wrap" { ... }`. `basename()` scans the path backward to the last separator, then the result is compared to "bun:wrap". The bare specifier `"bun:wrap"` is already in `BUN_ALIAS_MAP` (HardcodedModule.rs:705 `entry!("bun:wrap")`) and therefore short-circuited by `resolve_hook`'s `Alias::get` at line 5125 before `_resolve` is ever entered. The only input that could reach this check and match is a path literally ending in `/bun:wrap`, which is not a shape the runtime produces. Every real-world require pays the backward scan + 8-byte compare for nothing.
findings.evidence: jsc_hooks.rs:4848: `if bun_paths::basename(specifier) == b"bun:wrap" { *ret_path = b"bun:wrap"; return Ok(()); }`. HardcodedModule.rs:705 shows `entry!("bun:wrap")` in BUN_EXTRA_ALIAS_KVS, so `Alias::get(b"bun:wrap", Target::Bun, _)` returns Some — meaning resolve_hook:5125 already handles it. `bun_paths::basename` (paths/resolve_path.rs:2121) is a byte-by-byte reverse scan.
findings.proposed_fix: Replace with a cheap direct equality `if specifier == b"bun:wrap"` (to preserve any defensive intent without the scan), or drop the block entirely since resolve_hook already covers the bare form and nothing produces a path-suffixed `*/bun:wrap`.
findings.user_visible_impact: Every `require()`/`import()` that hits the filesystem resolver does an extra O(basename-length) backward scan (~8-15 byte ops) plus an 8-byte compare. Small per call, but multiplied by thousands of resolves on startup of large CJS apps.

findings.title: stmt.get() drains the entire result set after reading the first row
findings.problem: `jsSQLStatementExecuteStatementFunctionGet` constructs one result object, then enters `while (status == SQLITE_ROW) status = sqlite3_step(stmt);` to exhaust the cursor. For a SELECT that matches many rows, Bun steps through all of them (B-tree traversal, page decodes) even though the caller only asked for one. `sqlite3_reset()` is already called at the top of every subsequent execution, so draining is not required for correctness; `sqlite3_reset(stmt)` after building the first row would release the cursor immediately at O(1). better-sqlite3's `get()` resets after the first row rather than draining. (SQLite docs confirm DML-with-RETURNING completes all changes before the first row is emitted, so resetting after one row is safe there too.)
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the drain loop with `sqlite3_reset(stmt);` after the result object is built (mirroring better-sqlite3). If defensive draining is desired for future RETURNING semantics, gate it on `!sqlite3_stmt_readonly(stmt)` so plain SELECTs never drain.
findings.user_visible_impact: `db.query('SELECT * FROM big_table').get()` is O(rows_in_table) instead of O(1); on a 1M-row table this does ~1M unnecessary sqlite3_step() calls per .get().

findings.title: `url.searchParams` first-access allocates a '?'-prefixed search String only to immediately strip the '?'
findings.problem: `DOMURL::searchParams()` calls `URLSearchParams::create(search(), this)`. `URLDecomposition::search()` (URLDecomposition.cpp:221-225) does `fullURL.queryWithLeadingQuestionMark().toString()` — copies the URL then heap-allocates a substring String including the leading '?'. The URLSearchParams ctor (URLSearchParams.cpp:56-60) then checks `init.startsWith('?')` and feeds `StringView(init).substring(1)` to `parseURLEncodedForm`. So the '?' is added only to be detected and removed, and an intermediate String is allocated that `parseURLEncodedForm` (which takes StringView) never needed.
findings.evidence: DOMURL.cpp:151 `m_searchParams = URLSearchParams::create(search(), this);`. URLDecomposition.cpp:224 returns `fullURL.queryWithLeadingQuestionMark().toString()`. URLSearchParams.cpp:58 `m_pairs(init.startsWith('?') ? WTF::URLParser::parseURLEncodedForm(StringView(init).substring(1)) : WTF::URLParser::parseURLEncodedForm(init))`.
findings.proposed_fix: Add `static Ref<URLSearchParams> create(StringView query, DOMURL*)` that calls `parseURLEncodedForm(query)` directly, and have `DOMURL::searchParams()` pass `m_url.query()` (a StringView without '?') instead of `search()`. Saves one URL copy, one substring String allocation, and the `startsWith('?')` branch per first `url.searchParams` access.
