findings.user_visible_impact: `new URL(href).searchParams` does one extra String allocation and URL-struct copy per call; noticeable on request-routing hot paths that read searchParams for every incoming request.

findings.title: ws.send()/publish() probe asArrayBuffer via FFI before the common string path
findings.problem: `send()` checks `message_value.as_array_buffer(global_this)` first (an FFI call into `JSC__JSValue__asArrayBuffer` plus zeroing a 72-byte `ArrayBuffer::default()` out-param), then falls through to the string path. `publish()` does the same at line 793. The dominant call pattern is `ws.send(string)`, for which `asArrayBuffer` always returns false after an FFI round-trip. `message_value.is_string()` is an inline cell-header read (JSValue.rs:275/446) and would let the text path be taken with zero FFI before `to_js_string`.
findings.evidence: ServerWebSocket.rs:1070-1078 `if let Some(buffer) = message_value.as_array_buffer(global_this) { ... } { let js_string = message_value.to_js_string(global_this)?; ... }`; JSValue.rs:945-953 `pub fn as_array_buffer(self, global) { let mut out = ArrayBuffer::default(); if JSC__JSValue__asArrayBuffer(self, global, &mut out) { ... } }`; bindings.cpp:3061-3134 switches on cell type, `default: return false` for JSString. `is_string()` (JSValue.rs:275) and `js_type()` (JSValue.rs:446) are `#[inline]` header-byte reads with no FFI.
findings.proposed_fix: Reorder `send()`/`publish()` to test `if message_value.is_string() { /* text path */ } else if let Some(buffer) = message_value.as_array_buffer(global_this) { /* binary path */ } else { throw }`. This removes one FFI call and an `ArrayBuffer::default()` init from every string send without changing semantics (non-cell / non-string-non-buffer values still error identically).
findings.user_visible_impact: `ws.send("...")` inside a message handler — the hottest echo/broadcast pattern — currently makes an extra FFI call that always fails. Eliminating it trims a fixed per-send cost; measurable in `autobahn`-style small-string echo throughput.

findings.title: Buffer.concat stages every element into a MarkedArgumentBuffer even for dense JSArrays that cannot trigger the TOCTOU it guards against
findings.problem: To defend against index getters/proxies that mutate earlier elements (per the comment at lines 895-905), concat first copies every list element into a MarkedArgumentBuffer via forEachInArrayLike, then makes two more passes over that buffer (sum lengths, memcpy). For the overwhelmingly common input — a dense JSArray with Contiguous/Int32 storage — JSObject::tryGetIndexQuickly reads straight from the butterfly and never runs user JS, so the TOCTOU cannot occur, yet we still pay for: (a) a heap MarkedArgumentBuffer when list.length > 8 (inlineCapacity=8), (b) registering it with the GC mark set, and (c) an extra full pass over the list.
findings.evidence: Lines 906-923 unconditionally build `MarkedArgumentBuffer args` via `JSC::forEachInArrayLike(..., [&](JSValue element){ args.append(element); ... })`, then loop over `args` twice more at lines 935-945 and 981-989. MarkedArgumentBuffer = MarkedArgumentBufferWithSize<8> (vendor/WebKit/.../MarkedVector.h:532). forEachInArrayLike → getIndex → tryGetIndexQuickly fast-path returns directly from butterfly for contiguous arrays (JSObjectInlines.h:1433) with no user-JS side effects.
findings.proposed_fix: Before staging, detect the fast case: `auto* array = dynamicDowncast<JSArray>(listValue)` with a copyable indexing type (hasAnyContiguous(indexingType()) / Int32Shape) and no sparse map. In that case iterate `array->butterfly()->contiguous()` directly for both the length-sum and memcpy passes and skip the MarkedArgumentBuffer entirely. Fall through to the current generic path otherwise.
findings.user_visible_impact: Buffer.concat(list) with more than ~8 chunks (typical for stream collectors) does one extra O(n) pass plus a heap allocation and GC-mark-set insertion per call that could be elided; measurable on hot paths that concat many small chunks.

findings.title: DOMJIT fast-paths for ptr() and read.* are commented out — every call hits the slowpath
findings.problem: All 13 DOMJIT::Signature registrations for FFI.ptr and read.{u8,u16,u32,i8,i16,i32,i64,u64,f32,f64,ptr,intptr} are commented out (PR #14005 "disable most DOMJIT"). The JSFunction::create calls pass no signature, so DFG/FTL cannot lower these to a direct fast-path call. Every invocation, even in a hot JIT-tiered loop, goes through Reader__*__slowpathWrapper → Rust addr_from_args (is_number() check, length check, to_int32(), usize::try_from().expect()) → read_unaligned_at. The Rust source comment at /workspace/bun/src/runtime/ffi/FFIObject.rs:412-414 still claims the fast-path readers live on the C++ side, which is no longer true.
findings.evidence: ZigGeneratedCode.cpp:85-107: `// static const JSC::DOMJIT::Signature DOMJIT_u8_signature(...)` is commented, and the live code path is `JSFunction::create(vm, go, 2, "u8"_s, Reader__u8__slowpathWrapper, ImplementationVisibility::Public, NoIntrinsic);` with no signature argument. grep shows 13/13 DOMJIT::Signature declarations in this file are commented. git blame points to cf4e9cb69e "disable most DOMJIT (#14005)".
findings.proposed_fix: Re-enable the DOMJIT signatures (uncomment the fastpathWrapper JIT_OPERATION definitions and pass &DOMJIT_*_signature to JSFunction::create). If the original disable was due to a crash, gate re-enable behind a validated fix; at minimum re-enable the Reader__{u8,i8,u16,i16,u32,i32,f32,f64} cases which are pure unaligned loads. Also delete the stale comment at FFIObject.rs:412-414 so future readers don't assume the fast path exists.
findings.user_visible_impact: read.u8(ptr)/read.i32(ptr) in a tight loop is several-times slower than it was before DOMJIT was disabled — each call is a full host-function dispatch with argument type-checking instead of an inlined pointer-plus-offset load.

findings.title: `URL.parse()` routes through `wrap()` and performs a wrapper-cache HashMap lookup that can never hit
findings.problem: `jsDOMURLConstructorFunction_parseBody` returns via `toJS<IDLNullable<IDLInterface<DOMURL>>>(..., DOMURL::parse(...))`. `JSConverter<IDLInterface<T>>::convert` (JSDOMConvertInterface.h:80-83) calls free `toJS(..., DOMURL*)` which is `wrap()` (JSDOMWrapperCache.h:199-204): it first calls `getCachedWrapper(world, domObject)` — a `world.wrappers().get(ptr)` HashMap lookup — before falling back to `toJSNewlyCreated`. `DOMURL::parse` always adoptRefs a brand-new `DOMURL`, so the lookup is guaranteed to miss. The constructor (`JSDOMURLDOMConstructor::construct`, line 169) correctly uses `toJSNewlyCreated` and skips this lookup; `URL.parse()` does not.
findings.evidence: JSDOMURL.cpp:646 `toJS<IDLNullable<IDLInterface<DOMURL>>>(... DOMURL::parse(...))`. JSDOMConvertInterface.h:80 `return toJS(&lexicalGlobalObject, &globalObject, Detail::getPtrOrRef(value));`. JSDOMWrapperCache.h:201 `if (auto* wrapper = getCachedWrapper(globalObject->world(), domObject)) return wrapper;`. DOMURL.cpp:100-106 `parse` returns `adoptRef(*new DOMURL(...))`.
findings.proposed_fix: Change line 646 to use `toJSNewlyCreated<IDLNullable<IDLInterface<DOMURL>>>` (and, separately, fix `JSConverter<IDLNullable<T>>::convertNewlyCreated` in JSDOMConvertNullable.h:166-171 to forward to `JSConverter<T>::convertNewlyCreated` instead of `::convert`, which currently defeats the newly-created fast path for all nullable interfaces).
findings.user_visible_impact: `URL.parse(str)` does an extra never-hit HashMap lookup compared to `new URL(str)`, making the ‘faster because no try/catch’ API marginally slower than it should be.

findings.title: `write_with_formatting` scans the format string byte-by-byte instead of using SIMD memchr for '%'
findings.problem: When `console.log` has 2+ args and the first is a string, it is routed to `StringPossiblyFormatted` → `write_with_formatting`, which loops `while i < len { match slice[i] { b'%' => ..., _ => {} } i += 1; }`. For the extremely common case where the leading string contains no `%` (e.g. `console.log("request url:", url)`), this walks every byte with scalar compare instead of using `bun_core::strings::index_of_char(slice, b'%')` / memchr to jump to (or prove absence of) the next `%` in a handful of SIMD iterations. The trailing `write_all(slice)` is already bulk, only the scan is scalar.
findings.evidence: ConsoleObject.rs:2472-2729: `'outer: while i < len { ... match slice[i as usize] { b'%' => { ... }, _ => {} } i += 1; }` — no `index_of_char`/`memchr` anywhere in the file (grep for index_of_char|memchr in ConsoleObject.rs returns nothing).
findings.proposed_fix: Replace the per-byte increment with `match strings::index_of_char(&slice[i as usize..], b'%')` to advance `i` to the next `%` (or break when None). Same behavior, O(n/16) instead of O(n) on the no-`%` path.
findings.user_visible_impact: `console.log(longStringWithoutPercent, value)` (logging URLs, file paths, JSON fragments as labels) pays a scalar byte-by-byte scan of the label on every call; for a 1 KB label this is ~1000 iterations vs ~64 with SIMD memchr.
